questions.question: What are the three categories of outreach actions in the automation framework?

options.text: Always Automate (zero-judgment tasks), Automate with Human Gates (AI drafts, human approves), Never Automate (high-stakes moments)

questions.explanation: The three categories are: Category 1 (Always Automate) for repetitive zero-judgment tasks like DNS setup and bounce handling; Category 2 (Automate with Human Gates) for AI-drafted content that humans validate; Category 3 (Never Automate) for high-stakes moments like first replies, objection handling, and pricing discussions.

questions.question: Why should first replies from prospects NEVER be automated?

options.text: First replies are too short for AI to process
First replies reveal intent, objections, and buying signals that no AI can fully parse — and they're the moment trust is won or lost
Email tools don't support automated replies
First replies are usually spam and should be ignored

questions.explanation: First replies are the critical trust-building moment. They reveal intent, objections, questions, and buying signals that require human judgment to interpret. An automated response to a genuine question signals 'you're talking to a bot,' which destroys the relationship before it begins.

questions.question: What did the $127,000 mistake in the lesson illustrate about automation risk?

options.text: AI tools are too expensive for solo founders
A VP detected automated outreach and cancelled consideration of a $127K annual contract — proving that what you automate matters more than how well you automate
You should never use any automation
Enterprise prospects don't respond to email

questions.explanation: A Fortune 500 VP detected automated outreach and said 'This approach tells me everything I need to know about how you treat customers.' That single prospect represented $127K in annual revenue. The lesson: the better your automation gets, the more critical it becomes to know what should never be automated.

questions.question: DNS setup, warmup emails, and bounce handling are examples of tasks that should always be automated with no human review needed.
questions.explanation: True. DNS setup, warmup emails, list imports, bounce handling, and unsubscribe processing are Category 1 (Always Automate) tasks. They are repetitive, zero-judgment tasks where no human should waste time. The risk of error is low and easily correctable.

questions.question: List three outreach actions you currently automate (or plan to). For each, classify it into Category 1 (Always Automate), Category 2 (Automate with Human Gate), or Category 3 (Never Automate). If any are miscategorized in your current setup, explain what changes you need to make.
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response lists three specific outreach actions, correctly categorizes each with reasoning, and identifies any current miscategorizations. Look for understanding that high-stakes moments (first replies, pricing, objections) belong in Category 3, and that most AI-generated content belongs in Category 2 with human review gates.
